curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use rise/run to calculate the gradient of a straight line, reason about how changing x- and y-values changes steepness, and connect gradient to real-world rates of change and accessible design. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How does gradient help us describe, compare, and design slopes and rates of change in the world?

knowing_world.focus: Gradient measures steepness and direction of a line and can be calculated as rise/run (change in y divided by change in x). Students explore how moving points changes the gradient in real time.
key_concepts: Change in y over change in x
Effect of horizontal distance on gradient
Effect of vertical change on gradient
tools: Interactive sketchpad: drag two points and observe gradient updating
activities: Investigate how moving one point up/down affects gradient.
Investigate how changing horizontal distance affects gradient.
Find examples of lines with gradient 1 and -2 and justify how you know.

human_story.focus: Gradient connects to real-world flight paths and the history of mathematical contributions, highlighting Katherine Johnson’s NASA work and questions of access to powerful mathematical ideas.
justice_prompts: Why is it important that everyone gets access to powerful maths ideas like this?
Who benefits when we all understand?
activities: Watch a linked scene from Hidden Figures (in Canvas) and connect gradient to flight-path calculation.

expressive_self.focus: Students express what gradient feels and looks like through metaphor, art, or speaking, linking mathematical slope to personal energy and experience.
activities: Sketch a gradient-inspired image (e.g., in Pencil Spaces).
Record a short voice note or video about how it feels to see a gradient move.
Reflect: What kind of slope matches your current energy (steep climb, slow rise, downward glide)?

designed_world.focus: Gradient supports design decisions in the built world, especially safe and accessible slopes. Students use interactive tools to test constraints and model scenarios like ramps and cycles.
tools: Interactive sketchpad: explore constraints and build real-world scenarios using gradient values
design_challenges: Decide what gradient would work for a safe wheelchair ramp.
Decide what slope would be too steep for a hand-powered bike.
Model a real-world slope using the sketch and explain which constraints you changed and why.

regulation_supports: Inhale slowly as if climbing a steep slope; exhale like gliding down.
Trace a line with your finger or draw a slope in the air and notice how your body reacts.
wellbeing_prompts: Where did you feel flow in this lesson?

pathways.name: Final Task - Gradient Investigator
pathways.focus: Demonstrate understanding of gradient through interactive exploration evidence, creative expression, a design application, and reflection.
products: 3 screenshots or recordings from sketchpad explorations
A creative piece (drawing, simile, voice note, or video)
A ramp or slope design (sketch and/or calculation)
A reflection: “Where might I use this skill in the real world?”

required: Plot and read points on a coordinate grid
Work confidently with positive and negative numbers
Understand subtraction as ‘change’ between two values

builds_toward: Equation of a line (y = mx + c)
